topic: Use the gateway IP and how it interacts with kube-vip (ARP and BGP modes) for the 001 k3s-on-Proxmox spec.

starting_context.summary: Session 1 fixed the integration as: Packer template + OpenTofu (bpg/proxmox) clones + cloud-init k3s install with --cluster-init --tls-san=<VIP> + kube-vip DaemonSet + CSI Helm. VIP placement, gateway behaviour, and kube-vip mode selection were not yet decided.

ARP mode adds a VIP to a node interface that kubelet may wrongly autodetect as the node's InternalIP. Fix is to set --node-ip explicitly on kubelet and, when using Calico, set IP_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip and IP6_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip on calico-node.
Average ARP failover ~3s, dependent on switches forwarding gratuitous ARP. Some switches filter/learn gratuitous ARP across ports.

Default Proxmox install creates vmbr0 as a Linux bridge attached to the first NIC; host IP and gateway are on vmbr0. Guest NICs attach to vmbr0 like a software switch. Gratuitous ARP traverses vmbr0 transparently to the physical NIC and the upstream switch.

k3s default Flannel backend is vxlan. On --flannel-backend=host-gw, kube-vip ARP VIP traffic depends on direct L2 connectivity between all nodes (gateway must be on the same L2). --flannel-backend=vxlan is recommended for most setups because it does not assume any L3 topology on the underlay.

When using BGP without leader election, all nodes announce the VIP. kube-vip polls kube-apiserver health and withdraws the BGP route for a node whose apiserver is failing. Requires a BGP-speaking router as the gateway or adjacent.

tool_invocations.query: ARP mode cautions node-ip calico IP_AUTODETECTION_METHOD
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: VIP may be wrongly picked as node InternalIP; set --node-ip explicitly. For Calico, set IP_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip and IP6_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip.

tool_invocations.query: BGP mode, control-plane health check, ECMP
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: BGP mode advertises VIP to a configured peer; without leader election all nodes advertise and the router does ECMP; kube-vip can withdraw a node's BGP route on apiserver health-check failure.

tool_invocations.query: failover time, VIP preservation, gratuitous ARP across switches
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: typical ARP failover ~3s; PreserveVIPOnLeadershipLoss feature; IPv6 always removed immediately on leadership loss to prevent DAD failures.
Captured: routing-table mode injects routes into table 198 with proto 248. Useful when kube-vip is fenced by rp_filter or when ARP works only one-way.

tool_invocations.query: vmbr0 default Linux bridge gratuitous ARP VLAN
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: vmbr0 is a Linux bridge forwarding MAC frames transparently between guest vNICs and the physical NIC. STP off by default. bridge-disable-mac-learning 1 available since PVE 7.3.
Captured: default vxlan (no underlay L2 assumption); host-gw requires direct L2 between nodes; wireguard-native encrypts the pod overlay.

sources.title: kube-vip :: BGP mode (Control Plane Health Check)
kube-vip :: Troubleshooting ARP (failover times, VIP preservation)
Proxmox VE :: Network Configuration (vmbr0, VLAN, bond)
K3s Networking :: Basic Network Options (flannel backends)
Calico autodetection and kube-vip VIP interaction

alternatives_considered.option: ARP mode with VIP = the gateway's IP
pros: Minimal moving parts; single address for upstream
cons: The current gateway host (Proxmox PVE host) would lose the IP during kube-vip leadership transitions; ARP on the upstream switch risks flapping the entire VLAN
Conflicts with Proxmox management IP; do not repurpose the PVE host's gateway
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Don't redeclare PVE's gateway as kube-vip VIP. Use a free address in the same /24 as the gateway instead.

alternatives_considered.option: ARP mode with VIP in a different subnet than the gateway
cons: Requires upstream router to accept gratuitous ARP for an off-subnet IP; most L2 switches drop or filter this
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Pick a free IP inside the same L2 subnet as the gateway so gratuitous ARP works end-to-end.

alternatives_considered.option: BGP mode with the gateway as the BGP peer
pros: No gratuitous ARP; works across routed hops; BGP ECMP for control-plane HA without leader election bottleneck
cons: Requires the gateway (or an upstream router) to speak BGP; ARP mode is simpler for a homelab
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Default in the spec, but flagged as an ADR-worthy escape hatch when the gateway is a router with a BGP daemon (FRRouting, BIRD, OpenBGPD).

alternatives_considered.option: Use the ARP VIP as the default gateway for VMs
pros: No need for kube-vip LB on Service type; all east-west traffic funnels through the leader
cons: Single-leader bottleneck (matches the kube-vip docs warning on leaderElection-for-all-services), and the upstream default route changes whenever leadership changes, which can confuse Proxmox's own networking
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Keep the VIP strictly for kube-apiserver and Service LoadBalancer; let the existing gateway keep its role.

alternatives_considered.option: Routing-table mode (kube-vip adds routes to ip table 198)
pros: Sidesteps rp_filter or one-way ARP problems
cons: Requires policy routing setup on the host; not portable across cloud images; adds operational debt
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Default back to ARP unless rp_filter asymmetry is observed.

recommendation:
Reuse the existing PVE gateway subnet for the kube-vip VIP. Select a free host IP in the same /24 as vmbr0's gateway (e.g. gateway 192.168.10.1 -> VIP 192.168.10.40) and run kube-vip in ARP mode with --leaderElection. Concretely:

1. Define three OpenTofu variables: `network_gateway` (e.g. 192.168.10.1), `network_subnet` (e.g. 192.168.10.0/24), `kube_vip_vip` (a free IP inside the subnet). Reject any plan where `kube_vip_vip` is not in `cidrsubnet(network_subnet,...)`.
2. Pin the VIP into the cloud-init user-data and into every k3s install with `--tls-san=<kube_vip_vip>`. Document that the same value is used as the cluster's `kube-apiserver` endpoint.
3. For every node, set `--node-ip=<node_internal_ip>` explicitly in cloud-init so kubelet does not pick the VIP as InternalIP (avoids the kube-vip ARP 'Cautions' issue).
4. If the CNI is Calico (vendored-CSI plugin tolerates any CNI; PKE uses Cilium), set IP_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip on calico-node.
5. Generate the kube-vip DaemonSet manifest with `--interface <iface> --address $kube_vip_vip --controlplane --services --arp --leaderElection`. Hardcode the k3s-builtin `--node-ip` to the per-node address, not the VIP.
6. BGP upgrade path (separate ADR, not the default): document a `kube_vip_mode` variable ('arp' or 'bgp'). When set to 'bgp', the manifest switches to `--bgp --localAS <as> --bgpRouterID <id> --bgppeers <gateway-ip>:<as>::false`. The implementer must confirm the gateway speaks BGP (FRR/BIRD/OpenBGPD). Do not default to this.
rationale: Starting context constrains us to: (a) single Proxmox host, (b) k3s as the cluster manager, (c) default `vmbr0` Linux bridge with an upstream gateway, (d) kube-vip for HA, (e) Proxmox CSI for storage. The kube-vip ARP-mode Cautions warn that the VIP must not be picked as node InternalIP — solves by pinning --node-ip. Gratuitous ARP traverses the Linux bridge to the upstream switch transparently (per Proxmox Network_Configuration), so the only remaining requirement is that the VIP live in the same L2 subnet as the gateway. The router/gateway DOES NOT need to be reconfigured because gratuitous ARP merely updates its ARP cache; no static ARP entries are required. If the gateway is a Layer-3 BGP-capable router, BGP mode avoids the leader-election bottleneck, but that is an explicit upgrade decision (ADR), not the default for a homelab.
